Article.ArticleTitle: Revealing the material metabolism across residential water supply and drainage infrastructure renovations in China.

Abstract.AbstractText: Cities exhibit a metabolism governed by exchanges of materials and energy, yet the material dynamics of residential water infrastructure remain underexplored, particularly for China. Here, we conduct a bottom-up material flow analysis to quantify the stocks, flows, and associated greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from 2006 to 2030 in China's water infrastructure renovation. We compiled the material intensity from building-specific bills of quantities, covering 580,000 m2 of floor area across residences with various heights and construction periods, enabling spatially refined stock modeling through characteristic configurations instead of uniform averages. Results indicate that the material stocks of water supply and drainage infrastructure decline from 30.72 Mt in 2006 to 29.08 Mt in 2025. By material category, plastic pipes exhibit substantial stock growth and metal pipes show a gradual decline. Spatially, the Southeastern Economic-Dense and Water-Abundant Zone accounts for the largest material inflows (50.9%) and outflows (51.9%). And GHG emission accounting reveals the accumulated of 2.5 Mt of GHG in water infrastructure renovation and the predominant role of new pipeline production (56%). Across scenarios, emissions remain production-dominated (88%), with plastics account over 90% of production-stage GHG and 65% of treatment-stage GHG. Targeting subsidies, establishing construction and demolition (C&D) waste recycling system, promoting low-carbon pipe materials, and upgrading plastic recycling pathways are therefore raised to cut environmental impacts.
